finding: Adaptive learning representations depend on the structure of the environment; Bayesian learning processes interfere with task diagnosticity whereas decision-making facilitates it.
Two search strategies for information sampling each bias inferences in opposite directions: one strategy yields unbiased inferences for some hypotheses but biased inferences for others.
A feature-based model predicts appropriate effect-size distributions and reversal patterns in immediate memory without requiring strategic changes in encoding or storage buffer deployment.
Items previously subjected to retrieval-induced forgetting benefit more from relearning than items not previously forgotten.
Intermixed exposure to similar stimuli improves discriminability relative to blocked exposure, but this effect is not explained by trial spacing.
Phonological similarity between to-be-remembered items and irrelevant sounds disrupts free recall but not serial recall when category-cueing processes determine retrieval.
Semantic interference emerges both when responses are produced immediately and when responses are delayed, but frequency effects only appear in immediate responding.
Phonological processes used during reading aloud change adaptively across trials in response to predictable changes in stimulus category.
Learners acquire the relations they actively attend to regardless of spatial contiguity; joint attentional processing is sufficient for associative learning.
Priming dissociates from recognition and occurs without recognition; a single-system model accounts for this and other apparent evidence for multiple systems.
Bilinguals do not differ from monolinguals in active inhibition but show stronger reactive inhibition and better ability to maintain and use action goals.
Semantic relations between prime words automatically coordinate with the structure of arithmetic operations: addition facts are obligatorily activated only when primes denote aligned relations.
Activation spreads continuously from concepts to phonological forms during production; the amount of activation is limited and task-dependent, as shown by asymmetric distractor effects.
Retrospectively orienting attention to a single item in visual working memory reduces interference from test probes and increases resistance to updating.
Visual statistical learning operates over objects when feature dimensions covary perfectly, and over features when they are partially decoupled.
Directed forgetting is stronger for items strengthened via spaced presentations (context-based strengthening) than for items strengthened via processing time or depth (item-based strengthening).
Numbers in different notational systems are automatically translated into a common magnitude representation during comparison and same-different judgments.
Serial position functions for intrusion probes are parallel to those for positive probes, supporting a dual-process model with two retrieval routes.
Activated stereotypes and expectations at retrieval spontaneously generate illusory recollections consistent with those expectations, reported with subjective remembering.
Task switching imposes additional working memory costs; this cost scales with the number of switches and is independent of item maintenance load.
